---
title: "Initialising iLand landscapes"
author: "Quinn Asena"
date: today
from: markdown+emoji
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    embed-resources: true
theme:
  light: flatly
  dark: darkly
---

# Introduction

Full discloser: this document was written by Claude as an experiment. I have
included some edits.

This project constructs the spatial and ecological initialisation files required
to run the individual-based forest landscape model
[iLand](http://iland-model.org) across a set of boreal forest landscapes
spanning Alaska and Canada. The workflow takes raw remote sensing and climate
datasets and transforms them into the grid files, climate databases, species
initialisation tables, and environment files that iLand requires to simulate
forest dynamics.

The landscapes were originally selected by Lora Murphy and cover a range of
boreal forest conditions across the ABoVE (Arctic-Boreal Vulnerability
Experiment) study domain. See the selection and evaluation process here:
`Z:\project_data\na_boreal\Landscape building\Landscape selection`.
A single additional landscape at the Caribou-Poker
Creeks Research Watershed (CPCRW) in interior Alaska is appended to support
direct comparison with earlier single-site modelling work.

The pipeline is structured as ten sequential R scripts. Each script is
self-contained and operates on a consistent directory structure rooted at the
project working directory. Processing is parallelised across landscapes using
`future.apply` where independent per-landscape operations permit.

---

# File structure

Each landscape is stored in its own directory at the project root, named
`alaska_landscape_01`, `alaska_landscape_02`, ..., `canada_landscape_01`, etc.
Within each landscape directory the following structure is produced by the
pipeline:

```
landscape_name/
├── gis/
│   ├── env.grid.txt          # RU grid in ASCII format for iLand
│   ├── env.grid.tif          # RU grid as GeoTIFF
│   ├── env.file.txt          # RU grid as GeoTIFF
│   ├── stand_grid.txt        # Stand ID grid in ASCII format for iLand
│   ├── stand_grid.tif        # Stand ID grid as GeoTIFF
│   ├── DEM_processed.txt     # DEM in ASCII format for iLand
│   └── DEM_processed.tif     # DEM as GeoTIFF
├── databases/
│   └── <gcm><ssp>V3.sqlite   # Climate database for iLand
└── supporting_data/
    ├── ABoVE_LandCover/      # Cropped annual land cover tiles
    ├── ABoVE_Water/          # Cropped decadal surface water tiles
    ├── climate_link/         # Per-variable climate text files (linked version)
    │   ├── <gcm>/<ssp>/<var>/
    │   └── env.grid-climate.grid-link.txt
    └── gis/
        ├── env.grid.png              # Diagnostic plot of the RU grid
        ├── env.grid_disagg_10.tif    # 10m land cover grid
        ├── dem_lcp10.tif             # DEM projected to landscape grid
        ├── aspect_lcp10.tif          # Aspect (degrees)
        ├── hillshade_lcp10.tif       # Hillshade
        ├── permafrost_lcp10.tif      # Permafrost presence/absence
        └── init/
            └── forest_species_init_lc_yr<n>.tif  # Species codes per RU
```

Shared input data lives outside the landscape directories. Empirical stand
data and the sapling initialisation files produced by step 08 are stored in
`data/empirical/`. The selected landscape polygons are in `data/`.

---

# Data origins

## ABoVE land cover

Annual land cover classifications from the ABoVE project
([ORNL DAAC](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/data/catalog/ornl-cloud-annual-landcover-above-1691-1)),
covering 1984–2014 at approximately 30m resolution. The dataset uses a 15-class
scheme including evergreen, deciduous, mixed, and woodland forest classes, as
well as shrub, herbaceous, wetland, and barren types. A simplified 10-class
version is excluded. The land cover data are the primary input for RU grid
construction (step 02) and species initialisation (step 07).

## ABoVE surface water

Decadal surface water maps from the ABoVE project
([ORNL DAAC](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/data/catalog/ornl-cloud-decadal-water-maps-1324-1.1)),
covering three decades (1991, 2001, 2011). Used in step 07 to mask water-covered
cells from the species initialisation. This product was preferred over the water
class in the land cover data on the basis that it avoids erroneous overlap
between forested and water pixels.

## ABoVE study domain

The ABoVE reference grid defines the tiling system used across the land cover
and surface water datasets. Used in step 01 to verify spatial extent and
coordinate reference system.

## ArcticDEM

The ArcticDEM mosaic (v4.1, 10m resolution), accessed via the PGC STAC API.
Provides elevation data used to derive aspect and hillshade for each landscape.
Tiles are downloaded in step 05 with a 1000m buffer around the landscape extent
to support edge-effect-free aspect calculation.

## Downscaled climate

Lora has statistically downscaled daily climate data for the EC-Earth3-Veg,
GFDL-ESM4, TaiESM1, UKESM1-0-LL, and NorESM2-MM GCMs for SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5,
and SSP3-7.0.Variables include maximum and minimum temperature
(`tasmax`, `tasmin`), precipitation (`pr`), solar radiation (`rsds`), vapour
pressure (`vp`), and relative humidity (`hurs`). Data are accessed from
network storage and processed in step 03 into per-landscape text files before
being compiled into SQLite databases in step 04.

## Permafrost

A permafrost presence/absence raster sourced from network storage. Projected
and masked to each landscape in step 06. Used in step 07 to differentiate
black and white spruce on flat aspects.

## Soils

Rasters of soil depth, sand, silt, and clay fraction for the Alaska domain,
accessed from network storage. Projected to each landscape grid in step 10
and used to populate soil property fields in the iLand environment file.

## Empirical stand data

Post-fire regeneration data from two sources:

- **Johnstone**: black spruce, trembling aspen, and Alaskan birch stand density
  and species proportion data from interior Alaska.
- **Walker**: equivalent data from a broader set of Alaskan burn sites.

These are combined in step 08 to fit height distributions and generate the
sapling initialisation table.

---

# Workflow

The pipeline proceeds in ten steps. Steps 01–03 handle spatial and climate
data preparation; steps 04–10 build the iLand input files.

## Step 01 — Crop ABoVE data to landscape polygons

**Script:** `01_above_process.r`

The ABoVE land cover and surface water tiles cover the full study domain.
This step crops them to each landscape polygon and saves the results to
`supporting_data/ABoVE_LandCover/` and `supporting_data/ABoVE_Water/`
within each landscape directory.

Before cropping, NA values outside the landscape boundary are replaced with
a sentinel value of −1. This prevents outside-landscape NAs from being
confused with genuine NA values that exist within the landscape (e.g. water
bodies or unclassified pixels). A two-step crop-then-mask operation is applied:
first crop to the bounding box, then mask to the polygon boundary.

The surface water data are structured as one tile per decade rather than a
single multi-layer file. These are first grouped by decade, combined into a
per-decade VRT, then stacked into a single three-layer raster.

A single additional landscape at the Caribou-Poker Creeks Research Watershed
(CPCRW) is appended to the ABoVE landscape set at this step. CPCRW is
processed through the same pipeline as the other landscapes but requires
special handling in later steps because it falls outside the spatial domain of
the regional downscaled climate dataset for the other datasets (see step 03).

## Step 02 — Create the RU (resource unit) grid

**Script:** `02_env.grid_create.r`

iLand operates on a 100×100m resource unit grid. This step projects the first
land cover layer for each landscape to ESRI:102001 (Canada Albers Equal Area
Conic) at 100m resolution and assigns each non-NA cell a unique sequential
integer ID. This ID is the `env.grid` used throughout the pipeline to identify
individual RUs.

Two output formats are written: an ASCII grid (`.txt`) for direct use by iLand2.0
and a GeoTIFF for testing with iLand2.1. A 10m disaggregated version
is also produced by disaggregating the original 30m land cover raster by a
factor of 3, preserving the 15-class land cover values for use in steps 06
and 07.

## Step 03 — Process downscaled climate data

**Script:** `03_climate_create.r`

Daily climate variables are extracted from netCDF files and written to
tab-delimited text files, one per landscape per variable per year. Two
approaches are implemented:

**`process_climate`** reprojects and bilinearly interpolates the ~1000m
climate grid directly to the 100m RU grid. The output has one row per RU per
day, producing large files but a direct one-to-one RU-to-climate mapping.

**`process_climate_link`** avoids reprojecting the full climate dataset.
Instead, the landscape extent is buffered (4000m), the climate data are cropped
to that buffer and reprojected back to the landscape CRS, and a lookup table
is constructed mapping each RU to its corresponding climate grid cell. This
preserves the native climate resolution and is substantially faster for large
landscapes.

Both functions handle two variable-specific transformations: solar radiation
(`rsds`) is converted from W/m² to MJ/m²/day, and vapour pressure (`vp`) layer
names are standardised where the source file uses non-standard naming.
Leap years are accounted for by shifting day-of-year indices for days from
day 60 onwards.

CPCRW falls outside the spatial domain of the regional downscaled climate
dataset used for all other landscapes. Both functions detect this automatically
by cropping the first climate layer to the landscape extent and checking
whether all values are NA. If so, climate data are drawn from a separate
CPCRW-specific directory on network storage instead.

## Step 04 — Build climate SQLite databases

**Script:** `04_climate_create.r`

Reads the per-variable text files produced in step 03 and joins them into a
single data frame per landscape. Applies unit conversions and derives additional
variables:

- Temperature converted from Kelvin to Celsius
- Saturated vapour pressure (es) calculated separately for above- and
  below-freezing temperatures using the Magnus formula
- Actual vapour pressure deficit (VPD) derived as es − vp, floored at zero
- All negative radiation and vapour pressure values floored at zero

The processed data are split by `model.climate.tableName`
(a `landscape_climategrid` identifier) and written as individual tables into
an SQLite database. iLand looks up the climate table for each RU using this
naming convention.

## Step 05 — Download ArcticDEM tiles

**Script:** `05_dem_download.r`

Queries the PGC STAC API for ArcticDEM mosaic tiles intersecting each
landscape. The landscape extent is buffered by 1000m before the query to
ensure that the downloaded area extends beyond the landscape boundary —
this is necessary so that aspect calculation in step 06 can use valid
neighbouring cells at the landscape edge, avoiding a ring of NA values.

A skip condition checks whether tiles have already been downloaded before
initiating a new request, making it safe to re-run without duplication.
Downloaded assets include the DEM, hillshade, and date metadata tiles.

## Step 06 — Process DEM and terrain derivatives

**Script:** `06_dem_process.r`

Projects the downloaded DEM tiles to the 10m landscape grid and derives
terrain variables. The key processing order is:

1. Merge or VRT the downloaded tiles
2. Project to the buffered 10m landscape grid (`env_grid_10_buff`)
3. Compute aspect from the buffered, unmasked DEM — this ensures edge cells
   have valid neighbours on all sides
4. Mask both the DEM and aspect to the true landscape boundary
5. Project and mask hillshade (no buffer needed — it is a pre-computed product)
6. Project and mask permafrost

Outputs are the DEM, aspect, hillshade, and permafrost rasters in the
`supporting_data/gis/` directory. The DEM is additionally written in ASCII
format for iLand.

## Step 07 — Assign initial forest species

**Script:** `07_species_init.r`

Classifies each RU cell into an initial forest species based on ABoVE land
cover class, topographic aspect, and permafrost presence. The classification
rules are:

| ABoVE class | Condition | Species |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Evergreen) | Flat aspect, permafrost | Black spruce |
| 1 (Evergreen) | Flat aspect, no permafrost | White spruce |
| 1 (Evergreen) | North-facing | Black spruce |
| 1 (Evergreen) | All other aspects | White spruce |
| 4 (Woodland) | — | Black spruce |
| 2 (Deciduous) | South-facing | Aspen |
| 2 (Deciduous) | All other aspects | Birch |
| 3 (Mixed) | — | Mixed |
| 5–9 | — | Potential forest |

Cells with NA aspect (border cells where terrain calculation lacks full
neighbourhood) fall through to the class-only rules rather than remaining
unclassified.

Before classification, a 50m water proximity buffer is applied using the ABoVE
surface water product: cells within 50m of open water are masked out. This
avoids initialising forest on pixels that sit within or immediately adjacent
to water bodies.

Species are encoded as integers (1–6) and written as a GeoTIFF for use in
steps 09 and 10. The script is run twice: once for land cover year 1 (1984)
and once for year 31 (2014).

## Step 08 — Generate stand initialisation data

**Script:** `08_stand.grid_process.r`

Constructs the sapling initialisation table (`landscape_model_init.txt`) and
stand dictionary (`sapinit_dict.txt`) from empirical post-fire regeneration
data. This step does not operate per-landscape — it produces a single set of
files shared across all landscapes.

Stand composition is derived from the Johnstone and Walker datasets, which
provide species density and proportion data for black spruce, trembling aspen,
and paper birch. Stands are classified by dominant species using proportional
thresholds (≥75% for spruce and aspen, ≥50% for birch; all others classified
as mixed). The mixed category is represented three times in the dictionary —
once per constituent species — so that a mixed RU may draw from any of the
three height distributions.

Height distributions are fitted to empirical tree height data using maximum
likelihood estimation: gamma distributions for spruce and aspen, Weibull for
birch. Heights are sampled 20 times per stand to produce the initialisation
table, with age fixed at 11 years following the original model setup.

White spruce (*Picea glauca*, Pigl) is added as a separate forest type by
duplicating the black spruce stands with adjusted density (7800–16000 stems/ha),
height offset (+1m), and age (+15 years).

The stand dictionary maps forest class codes (from step 07) to stand_id ranges,
enabling step 09 to randomly assign stand IDs to landscape pixels.

## Step 09 — Create the stand grid

**Script:** `09_stand.grid_create.r`

Assigns a random stand ID from the appropriate species pool to every forested
RU cell. The stand dictionary from step 08 defines the valid `stand_id` range
for each forest class. Sampling is done with replacement, scattering stand IDs
across the landscape so that iLand initialises a spatially heterogeneous forest
rather than large patches of identical stand structure.

Potential forest cells (class 6) are assigned a value one above the maximum
stand ID, placing them outside the range of any real stand while preserving
their identity as recruitable pixels. Non-forest and water cells are set to
−9999 (the iLand no-data value). Output is written as both GeoTIFF and ASCII.

## Step 10 — Build the environment file

**Script:** `10_env.file_create.r`

Assembles the iLand environment file, which provides per-RU soil properties,
carbon and nitrogen pool initialisations, and the climate table name that iLand
uses to look up climate data. Two versions are produced corresponding to the
two climate approaches in step 03:

- `build_env_file`: uses the interpolated climate grid; the climate table name
  is formed from the landscape name and the RU's `env.grid` ID.
- `build_env_file_link`: uses the linked climate grid; the climate table name
  is formed from the landscape name and the corresponding climate grid cell ID
  from the lookup table.

Soil properties (depth, sand, silt, clay fraction) are projected from the AK
soil rasters to the RU grid. Carbon and nitrogen pools are initialised from
species-specific empirical ranges:

- Young labile carbon drawn from uniform distributions stratified by forest
  type (spruce/mixed/broadleaved), with C:N ratios applied to derive nitrogen
- Young refractory carbon set to fixed species-specific values
- Soil organic matter carbon fixed at 35,000 g/m² with a C:N ratio of 20
- Moss biomass drawn from species-specific ranges (highest for black spruce,
  near zero for broadleaved types)

All numeric fields are rounded before output. The final file is written as
a tab-delimited text file in the landscape `gis/` directory.

---

# Outputs for iLand

The following files are the direct inputs consumed by iLand for each landscape:

| File | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `env.grid.txt` | `gis/` | RU grid (ASCII) |
| `stand_grid.txt` | `gis/` | Stand ID grid (ASCII) |
| `DEM_processed.txt` | `gis/` | Digital elevation model (ASCII) |
| `<gcm><ssp>V3.sqlite` | `databases/` | Climate database |
| `env.file_link.txt` | `gis/` | Environment file |
| `landscape_model_init.txt` | `data/empirical/` | Sapling initialisation table |
 origins

## Data out

## Data out
